We've covered lots of knowledge points and integrated scraping into a Discord Bot. But in real-world we can't always run a Discord Bot from local. So next we'll cover server and host knowledge — this post is relatively simple, a brain break.
Many services we use rely on servers — Google, Facebook, Instagram, X (formerly Twitter), etc. To put it simply...

Huh? So what's the difference from our home computer? Actually a lot. Your home computer typically gets shut down when not in use, but servers don't — they have to run 24/7. Plus, server hardware specs are usually higher because they handle more workload.
Servers can be confusing because they're divided into two types:
Even though they're collectively called servers, they're easy to mix up.
Let's start with hardware servers. They usually include an operating system, and they're easier to understand — they're really similar to your home computer, just with better hardware specs.
You might say your home computer specs aren't bad either — so why does it lose to a server?

The main reason is different purposes. Home computers are mainly for personal use — gaming, media playback, web browsing, etc. So home computers focus more on graphics card and RAM, since they need more graphics processing and memory.

Servers, on the other hand, are oriented toward multi-user use — Google, Facebook, Instagram, X (formerly Twitter), etc. So servers need stronger CPUs and storage to handle more workload and store more data.

Home computer OSes are usually Windows, MacOS, etc. But server OSes are different — Windows Server, Ubuntu Server, CentOS, etc. So server OSes also differ from home computer OSes (which are mostly Windows or MacOS).
Although home computers and servers are very similar, they're designed for different purposes.
Hardware servers come in basically four form factors — rack-mounted, tower, blade, cabinet, etc. I won't elaborate — look it up if interested.
Software servers refer to the software running on the server. The server's purpose changes based on the software, so software servers can include:
Software servers operate differently. Web servers use HTTP for data transfer, while mail servers use SMTP — so software servers also operate differently.
The relationship between Node.js and servers

Why are we specifically covering servers? As mentioned in the intro: the knowledge from previous chapters all runs on local. But in practice, we can't always run on local — so we'll deploy Node.js to a server and have the server run our programs.
So many services run our programs through servers. As I mentioned on Day 1...
"Node.js is a JavaScript Runtime built on top of
. It lets us use JavaScript for server-side programming. JavaScript isn't limited to browsers anymore — it can run on the server too."
That's why I'm specifically dedicating a post to servers — in the next chapters we'll deploy Node.js to a server and run our programs through it.
Of course I won't ask you to set up a server from scratch, so the next post introduces using cloud services to deploy our Node.js programs. See you in the next one.
